coredata.dc:title: Water sensitized and highly bright multicolor Ni-doped carbon dots nanoarchitectonics in polar solvents towards stable flexible films
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Xuanqi
Shi, Wenbin
Yang, Ping
coredata.dc:description: To address the issues of low photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs), broad PL spectra, and poor stability, multicolor carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by using different precursors. Blue-emitting CDs were created via a solvothermal synthesis by using citric acid and ethylenediamine while green-emitting CDs were fabricated using O-phenylenediamine. Ni doping further increased the PLQYs and stability of CDs. Ni-doped CDs (Ni-CDs) with blue- and green-emitting were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal method. The PL peak wavelengths of Ni-doped blue CDs (Ni-BCDs) and Ni-doped green CDs (Ni-GCDs) were located at 450 and 540 nm, respectively. After Ni-doping, the average particle size of green-emitting CDs was increased from 2.35 to 2.65 nm. Compared with undoped CDs, Ni-CDs exhibited narrow PL spectra, high PLQYs, and long fluorescent lifetime. The PLQYs of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were increased to 83.92 and 35.60 %, respectively. Interestingly, the PL peak wavelengths of Ni-doped CDs revealed a red-shift with increasing the solvent polarity (e.g. acetone, acetonitrile, N, N-dimethylformamide, tetrahydrofuran, dimethyl sulfoxide, ethanol, methanol, and H2O). In addition, the PL peaks of green-emitting Ni-CDs generated a large red-shift with increase the ratio of water in organic solvents compared with blue one. Namely, solid Ni-GCDs powders also revealed water dependent PL. After incorporating the CDs in flexible films, Ni-GCDs still revealed high brightness and stability. These results supplied important possibility for the application of multicolor CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ni-doping
Water sensors
Flexible film
Photoluminescence

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of trace water in organic solvents

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan
National Natural Science Function of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by the projects from the National Natural Science Function of China, PR China, (grant no. 51972145), the Independent Innovation Team from Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau, Jinan, China (grant no, 2021GXRC109), and Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan, China (XKY2118).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation process of Ni-doped CDs (Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs).

TEM images of samples (a, b) G-CDs and (c, d) Ni-GCDs. The insets in (a) and (c) show the size distributions of G-CDs and Ni-GCDs samples, respectively (the number of particles used to evaluate the size distribution were 100). Insets in (b) and (d) show the TEM image of a particle of G-CDs and Ni-GCDs samples with well-developed lattice fronges.

(a, b) XRD patterns of samples B-CDs, Ni-BCDs, G-CDs and Ni-GCDs. (c, d) FTIR spectra of samples B-CDs, Ni-BCDs, G-CDs and Ni-GCDs (e) XPS survey spectrum of Ni-GCDs sample. High-resolution (f) C 1 s, (g) N 1 s, (h) O 1 s, and (i) Ni 2p spectra of Ni-GCDs sample.

(a, d) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and Ni-CDs samples. (b, e) PL spectra of CDs and Ni-CDs samples. (c, f) Fluorescent decay plots of CDs and Ni-CDs samples. Inset in (a, d) show the pictures of samples CDs and Ni-CDs under room light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) irradiation.

(a, d) PL evolution of samples Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs with time by 365 nm UV light irradiation. (b, e) PL evolution of samples Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs with temperature. (c, f) PL intensity evolution of samples Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs before and after heat treatment and 365 nm UV light irradiation.

Pictures of (a) Ni-BCDs and (c) Ni-GCDs dispersed in THF, AC, DMF, DMSO, ACN, EtOH, MeOH, EG and H2O irradiated under room light (top) and 365 nm UV light (bottom). PL spectra of (b) Ni-BCDs and (d) Ni-GCDs in different solvents.

UV–vis absorption spectra of samples (a) Ni-BCDs and (d) Ni-GCDs in different solvents. PL spectra of samples (b) Ni-BCDs and (e) Ni-GCDs at different water contents of THF. (c, f) Linear relationship of emission peak versus water content in THF. The insets in (c) and (f) show the pictures of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples dispersed in THF with various water contents under 365 nm UV light irradiation.

(a) PL spectra of Ni-GCDs sample compared with Ni-GCDs film. (b) PL evolution of Ni-GCDs film irradiated by 365 nm UV light. (c) PL evolution of Ni-GCDs film with time. (d) CIE coordinates of sample Ni-GCDs film. Inset in (a) shows the picture of curled Ni-GCDs film under 365 nm UV light irradiation. The Inset in (c) shows the pictures of Ni-GCDs film under room light (top) and 365 nm UV light irradiation (bottom).
PL properties of samples CDs and Ni-CDs.
Components B1 and B2, time constants τ1 and τ2, and average lifetime τaverage, of doped and undoped samples.

head.ce:title: Water sensitized and highly bright multicolor Ni-doped carbon dots nanoarchitectonics in polar solvents towards stable flexible films

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To address the issues of low photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs), broad PL spectra, and poor stability, multicolor carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by using different precursors. Blue-emitting CDs were created via a solvothermal synthesis by using citric acid and ethylenediamine while green-emitting CDs were fabricated using O-phenylenediamine. Ni doping further increased the PLQYs and stability of CDs. Ni-doped CDs (Ni-CDs) with blue- and green-emitting were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal method. The PL peak wavelengths of Ni-doped blue CDs (Ni-BCDs) and Ni-doped green CDs (Ni-GCDs) were located at 450 and 540 nm, respectively. After Ni-doping, the average particle size of green-emitting CDs was increased from 2.35 to 2.65 nm. Compared with undoped CDs, Ni-CDs exhibited narrow PL spectra, high PLQYs, and long fluorescent lifetime. The PLQYs of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were increased to 83.92 and 35.60 %, respectively. Interestingly, the PL peak wavelengths of Ni-doped CDs revealed a red-shift with increasing the solvent polarity (e.g. acetone, acetonitrile, N, N-dimethylformamide, tetrahydrofuran, dimethyl sulfoxide, ethanol, methanol, and H2O). In addition, the PL peaks of green-emitting Ni-CDs generated a large red-shift with increase the ratio of water in organic solvents compared with blue one. Namely, solid Ni-GCDs powders also revealed water dependent PL. After incorporating the CDs in flexible films, Ni-GCDs still revealed high brightness and stability. These results supplied important possibility for the application of multicolor CDs.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared with semiconductor quantum dots, the broad photoluminescent (PL) spectra, low PL quantum yields (PLQYs), and poor stability of carbon dots (CDs) limited their applications [1–5]. However, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and low cost make CDs with significant advantages. Thus, it is expected to create CDs with tunable PL, high brightness, and improved stability for the study of CDs [6–10]. Various methods have been developed to face such issues. For example, Xu et al. isolated short tubular carbon and fluorescent nanoparticles from soot in the process of purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes [11]. The term CDs was introduced by Sun et al. in 2006 to describe surface passivated nano-carbon particles [12], in which the concept of CDs was first proposed. After long-term development, the practical application of CDs has been extensively explored in various fields including bioimaging [13–16], light-emitting diodes (LED) [17,18], photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [19], ion detection [20,21], as well as other related fields. The PLQYs and stability of CDs have been greatly improved compared with the initial discovery.

The PLQYs and stability served as crucial metrics for assessing the optical characteristics of CDs. Many ways were developed to enhance the PLQYs and stability of CDs, such as heteroatomic doping and surface modification [22–25]. Surface modification was relatively trouble some because of required two or more steps. However, the synthesis of heteroatom-doped CDs could be achieved through a simply and efficient one-step solvothermal method. Currently, studies had extensively reported that hetero-atom-doped could effectively enhance the PLQYs and stability of CDs [26–28]. The process of heteroatomic doping involved the incorporation of nonmetallic and metallic elements. The interaction between heteroatoms and carbon atoms, as well as the interatomic forces, contributed to the alteration of the surface chemical structure of CDs [29]. The introduction of heteroatoms modified the band gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of CDs [30]. The surface and carbon core states affected the luminescence of CDs. The doping of heteroatoms might change surface chemical functional groups of CDs, further affect carbon core state of CDs, thus affect the luminescence properties of CDs [31,32].

Currently, literature reported various metal doping approaches, such as Mn, Ni, Cu, and Zn doping. The large radius of metal ions compared to nonmetallic ions results in the alteration of electron density in CDs when doped with metal ions, consequently leading to changes in the optical properties of CDs. In fact, studies had demonstrated that metal ions doping could effectively enhance the PL intensity and PLQYs of CDs. For example, Xu et al. reported on Zn-doped CDs using ZnCl2 as a precursor [33]. By adjusting reaction time, the blue-emitting Zn-CDs with PLQYs up to 34.6 % were successfully prepared. Liu et al. introduced Mn ions into the network of CDs and successfully prepared Mn-doped yellow-emitting CDs, the PLQYs of Mn doped CDs increased by 22.6 % [34]. Wu et al. successfully synthesized Ni-doped CDs with fluorescence range of 513 to 612 nm through precise adjustment of reaction conditions [29]. The incorporation of Ni effectively enhanced the fluorescence intensity and induced a redshift in PL spectra. Kong et al. utilized citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors to synthesize Ni-doped blue-emitting CDs, achieving a high PLQY of 54.7 %, which was increased 14 % compared with undoped one [35]. Zhang et al. used m-phenylenediamine as carbon precursors and NiCl2·6H2O as a dopant to synthesize green-emitting Ni-CDs with a PLQY of 30.5 % [36]. Therefore, metal ions doping was expected to improve the PLQYs and stability of CDs.

In this paper, different precursors were used to synthesize blue- and green-emitting CDs to overcome low PLQYs, broad PL spectra, and poor stability by Ni doping. Ni doping was employed to enhance the PLQYs and stability of CDs. A solvothermal synthesis was developed to introduce Ni into the carbon network of CDs. Consequently, Ni-doped CDs (Ni-CDs) with blue- and green-emitting were successfully prepared. Ni doping just increased the PL intensity rather than to change the PL peak of CDs. The PL peak wavelengths of Ni-doped blue-emitting CDs (Ni-BCDs) and green-emitting CDs (Ni-GCDs) were 450 and 540 nm, respectively. Compared with undoped CDs, Ni-CDs exhibited high PLQYs, and narrowed PL spectra. The PLQY of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were 35.6 % and 83.92 %, respectively. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of PL spectra of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were 75.57 and 92.80 nm, respectively. Ni-GCDs were embedded in PVA matrix to prepare bright green-emitting flexible films. When Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were dispersed in various polar solvents, the PL color of the CDs changed. The PL peak wavelengths of Ni-CDs gradually red-shifted as the relative polarity of the solvent increased. With increasing the amount of water, the PL peaks were gradually red shifted. These results suggest that the CDs can be used in various polar solvent environments to get bright PL.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD, 99.5 %), citric acid (CA, 99.7 %), nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 99.7 %) and ethylene glycol (EG, 99.5 %) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylene diamine anhydrous (EDA, 99 %) was supplied by Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Acetone (AC, 99.5 %) was purchased from Yantai Yuandong Fine Chemicals Co., LTD (China). Acetonitrile (ACN, 99.5 %), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %), tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99 %), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.5 %), ethanol (EtOH, 99.7 %) and methanol (MeOH, 99.5 %) were supplied by Tianjin Fuyu Chemical Co., Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (Mw = 44.05) was purchased from Aladdin. Deionized water came from Millipore system (SYNSV0000, Millipore, France). All chemicals were directly used without any additional treatment.

Synthesis of blue-emitting CDs. CDs were prepared by a solvothermal synthesis at 200 ºC for 5 h. Typically, 100 mg of CA and 0.1 mL of EDA were dissolved in 20 mL of EtOH with ultrasonication for 15 min to ensure solute homogeneity. The resulting homogeneous solution was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the sample was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 5 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to obtain a transparent blue-emitting solution CDs colloidal solution named as B-CDs.

Synthesis of green-emitting CDs. The synthesis method of green-emitting CDs was optimized on the basis of previous work [37]. The synthesis method was the same as that of B-CDs except that the solute and reaction conditions. 10 mg of OPD was dissolved in EtOH and solvothermal treated at 180 °C for 8 h. The obtained transparent green-emitting colloidal solution was named as G-CDs.

Synthesis of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs. The synthesis process of Ni-doped CDs was similar with undoped CDs except Ni precursor addition. Namely, the synthesis of Ni-doped luminescent blue- and green-emitting CDs were finished as follows. 2 and 1.5 mg of NiCl2·6H2O were respectively introduced as an additional precursor for blue and green emitting Ni-doped CDs named as Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of trace water in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: 10 μL of Ni-CDs solution was dissolved in 1 mL of organic solvents THF and ACN with different water contents (0–100 %). Then, the PL spectra of the mixed solutions were tested at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The linear relationship between PL peak wavelengths and water content was established.

1 g of PVA was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water with heating and stirring to prepare a PVA solution. Subsequently, the Ni-GCDs solution was mixed with the PVA solution. The mixture solution was dropped onto a clean glass plate and dried at room temperature for 5 h. Finally, the uniform transparent green-emitting film sample was obtained by stripping the dry Ni-GCD film from the glass substrate.

The morphology of CDs samples was tested using through transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Titan 300, America). The crystal structure of CDs samples was analyzed using X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Smart Lab 9KW, Rigaku, Japan). The CDs samples were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) using Nicolet 380 (Thermo Electron, America). The elemental components of CDs samples were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCA 3000, Thermo Fisher ESCALAB Xi, America). The absorption spectra of samples were examined using UV–visible (vis) spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-4100, Japan). The PL spectra of CDs samples were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan). The fluorescence decay plots of CDs samples were analyzed by Jobin Yvon IBH fluorescence lifetime analyzer (Horiba, Japan). The CDs samples were exposed to WFH-204B UV analyzer (Shanghai Chitang Industry, China) for irradiation.

Fig. 1 shows the preparation scheme of CDs. The blue-emitting CDs (B-CDs) utilized CA as carbon source and EDA as nitrogen source while the green-emitting CDs (G-CDs) using OPD as precursors. Using ethanol as a solvent, N-doped CDs (B-CDs) were obtained. To investigate the impact of Ni doping on the PL of CDs, the doping amount of Ni was adjusted, as shown in Fig. S1 (see Supporting Information). Ni-doped CDs (Ni-CDs) were prepared successfully by determining the proper doping ratio. The experimental data shown that introduction of Ni element did not change fluorescence color of CDs.

The morphology of G-CDs and Ni-GCDs samples were characterized by TEM observation. Fig. 2 shows the TEM images of green-emitting CDs samples before and after Ni doping, suggesting that monodispersed G-CDs and Ni-GCDs samples revealed a spherical morphology (Fig. 2a and 2c). The particle sizes of G-CDs and Ni-GCDs samples were 2.35 ± 2.5 nm and 2.65 ± 2.5 nm, respectively as shown in the insets in Fig. 2a and 2c. Ni doping caused the particle size increased. This is due to the introduction of Ni to increase the degree of dehydration carbonization of CDs. In addition, the lattice spacing of G-CDs and Ni-GCDs samples were 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon (Fig. 2b and 2d). The result confirms that Ni-doped CDs revealed high crystallinity [38].

To further investigate the microstructure, composition, and phase of CDs and Ni-CDs samples, XRD, FTIR and XPS analysis was performed. The XRD patterns of samples are presented in Fig. 3 a and 3b, suggesting a broad peak at approximately 24° was observed for both B-CDs and Ni-BCDs samples. This peak corresponded to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [39]. The intensity of the XRD peak of sample Ni-BCDs increased after Ni-doping. This phenomenon is ascribed that Ni-doping induced increased degree of graphitization of CDs. The FTIR spectra of samples are shown in Fig. 3c and 3d. Ni-CDs samples revealed similar functional group components with undoped CDs as indicated in Fig. 3c and 3d. The stretching vibration bands at 3350–3550 and 2800–3000 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of NH/OH bond and -CH2 and CC bonds. CDs samples exhibited polar functional groups. It is confirmed that CDs samples revealed well dispersibility in polar solvents. The stretching vibration band at 1600–1680 and 1500–1600 cm−1 was associated with the C = N/C = O, C = C and COOH bonds. Notably, the C = N/C = O bond peak of Ni-BCDs shifted from 1640 to 1655 cm−1, while the Ni-GCDs shifted from 1631 to 1643 cm−1 upon Ni doping, indicating the formation of Ni-N bond. The results indicated the formation of an bonding between Ni2+ and CDs, providing evidence for the successful doping of Ni element into the CDs [35,36,40]. The elemental composition of Ni-GCDs was analyzed by XPS. Ni-GCDs was composed of elements C, N, O and Ni with characteristic peaks at 285, 399, 532 and 856 eV with atomic ratios of 83.21, 9.95, 5.64 and 1.20 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3e. Fig. 3f–3i show the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ni-GCDs. The C 1 s spectrum of Ni-GCDs in Fig. 3f was divided into 3 peaks at 284.4, 285.6 and 288.0 eV, corresponding to CC/C = C, CN/CO and C = O bonds, respectively [41]. The N 1 s spectrum of Ni-GCDs sample was fitted into 3 peaks located at 399.0, 399.8, and 401.8 eV, attributing to Pyridine N, Pyrrole N, and Graphite N bonds respectively (Fig. 3g). The O 1 s spectrum of Ni-GCDs was observed around at 531.6 and 533 eV, corresponding to C = O and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 3h). In the Ni 2p spectrum, the peaks at 853.7 and 871.4 eV can be attributed to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the presence of Ni element in Ni-GCDs sample (Fig. 3i). Additionally, two weak peaks observed at 859.3 and 877.4 eV in the Ni 2p spectrum, corresponding to nickel oxide. This is ascribed to the exposure of Ni-GCDs in ambient condition, resulting in the oxidation of Ni.

To compare the dispersion of CDs before and after Ni-doping, CDs samples were dispersed in the ethanol solution with the same proportion. Both of CDs and Ni-CDs samples revealed excellent dispersion in ethanol as shown in the illustrations in Fig. 4 a and 4d. The CDs colloidal solution revealed uniform and bright blue- and green-emitting under irradiation of UV light. The inserted pictures in Fig. 4a and 4d clearly demonstrated that the brightness of CDs became significantly enhanced after Ni doping. Then, the optical properties of CDs samples were assessed through a series of experiments. Fig. 4a and 4d show the absorption spectra of samples. The result illustrated a noticeable shift in the absorption peak of Ni-CDs samples compared with CDs samples. Sample B-CDs exhibited absorption peaks at 240 and 360 nm, attributing to the n-π* transition of the C = O bond. Although the absorption peak position of samples Ni-BCDs remained unchanged, their intensity was obvious increased. Ni-GCDs and G-CDs samples revealed absorption peaks at 240, 270 and 380 nm which were attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C and the n-π* transition of C = O, respectively. Ni doping resulted in structural change of CDs and increased the absorption value. Fig. 4b and 4e show the PL spectra of CDs samples. The PL peak positions of B-CDs and G-CDs were located at 450 and 540 nm respectively, which are same with those of Ni-CDs samples while the PL intensity was greatly improved. The PL intensity of Ni-BCDs increased by 136 %, and Ni-GCDs sample increased by 484 %. Fig. S2 (see Supporting Information) shows PL spectra of CDs and Ni-CDs samples at different excitation wavelengths. The PL peak wavelengths of CDs and Ni-CDs samples were 310–390 and 360–480 nm, with an increment of 20 nm, respectively. Samples CDs and Ni-CDs exhibited excitation wavelength independent PL. The luminescent properties of CDs were influenced by both surface and core situation. The PL spectra of CDs and Ni-CDs samples show a single PL peak, corroborating the monochromatic luminous properties of CDs and Ni-CDs.

After Ni doping, the PL spectra of the sample became narrow as illustrates in Table 1 . The FWHM of PL spectra of samples Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs decreased about 1 nm. The result demonstrated that the effective enhancement of PL intensity of CDs through Ni-doping. The PLQYs of B-CDs, Ni-BCDs, G-CDs and Ni-GCDs samples were 71.35, 83.9, 30.4, and 35.6 %, respectively in the case of their excited wavelengths at 370 and 381 nm. To investigate the change of fluorescent lifetime of CDs samples before and after Ni doping, the fluorescent decay plots are shown in Fig. 4e and 4f. The fluorescent decay curves of samples were fitted with a double exponential attenuation function. Table 2 illustrates the short component τ1 and long component τ2 and corresponding weight coefficients B1 and B2. The τ1 and τ2 correspond respectively to the recombination process of the core states and surface states of CDs. The fluorescent lifetimes of B-CDs, Ni-BCDs, G-CDs and Ni-GCDs samples were 5.85, 8.13, 17.54 and 17.74 ns, respectively. Ni doping in CDs accelerated the process of electron radiative recombination, but inhibited electron non-radiative recombination. The result indicates that Ni doping eliminates the surface defects of CDs. The doping of metal elements has been demonstrated to significantly enhance the luminous efficiency of CDs.

The stability of the material was importance and had a direct impact on practical application. To assess the stability of Ni-CDs samples, the photostability and thermal stability were tested. Ni-CDs samples were continuously exposed to 365 nm UV light for 90 min, with an interval of 15 min for PL measurement (Fig. 5 a and 5d). The PL peak position of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples remained unchanged after 365 nm UV light irradiation within 90 min, suggesting Ni-CDs samples revealed high stability against photobleaching. In addition, the stability of Ni-CDs samples with temperature was tested. Fig. 5b and 5e show the PL evolution of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples after heated to 78 °C. Fig. 5c and 5f show the PL intensity evolution of the light and thermal stability PL intensity of Ni-CDs samples. After irradiation for 90 min, the PL intensity of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples remained at 80 and 121 % of initial value, respectively. After heating to 78 °C, the PL intensity of samples remained at 108 and 80 % of the initial value, respectively. This confirms Ni-CDs samples with excellent stability, providing significant support for the practical application.

Interestingly, as-prepared Ni-CDs samples revealed solvent dependent PL as shown in Fig. 6 . After re-dispersing samples in THF, AC, DMF, DMSO, ACN, EtOH, MeOH, EG and H2O, Ni-CDs exhibited excellent solubility without any precipitation, in which transparent solution CDs samples were obtained as shown in the top part in Fig. 6a and 6c. Bright PL with red-shifted PL peaks from these re-dispersed samples were observed under 365 nm UV light irradiation as shown in the bottom in Fig. 6a and 6c. This is ascribed to the polarity od solvents. Reichardt proposed the concept of parameter of solvent polarity EN T in 1994 [42], in which the polarity of 362 solvents were studied. With the change of solvent polarity EN T, the solvent-dependent PL of Ni-CDs samples were observed. The polarity of solvents is listed in Table S1 (see Supporting Information). With the increase of parameter of solvent polarity EN T, the absorption peaks of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples in different solvents exhibited a red shift (Fig. 7 a and 7d), indicating that Ni-CDs samples had different surface states. The PL properties of Ni-CDs were closely related to surface functional groups. The surface state of Ni-CDs changed when Ni-CDs were dispersed in different polar solvents, which affected the luminescence of Ni-CDs. This phenomenon was reflected in the PL spectra, as shown in Fig. 6b and 6d. With increasing solvent polarity, red-shifted PL spectra were observed. The relative polarity parameters of the solution and a red shift degree of PL spectra were presented in Table S1 (see Supporting Information). Samples Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs red-shifted by 26 and 56 nm, respectively. In addition, the PL peak was linearly fitted, as shown in Fig. S3 (see Supporting Information). The correlation coefficients R2 for Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were 0.8643 and 0.9685, respectively. The red shift of the PL spectra was caused by interaction between the surface functional groups present of CDs and the organic solvent. These results clearly indicated that Ni-CDs were solvent-dependent. The absorption and PL spectra of Ni-CDs in various solvents exhibited a red shift. This phenomenon was attributed to the formation of hydrogen bond complexes between the ground state molecule of the Ni-CDs and the solvent molecule [43,44]. In addition, the discoloration of the solution could also be analyzed by protonated and aprotonated solvents. THF, AC, DMF, DMSO and ACN are aprotonated solvent, and EtOH, MeOH, EG and H2O are protonated solvents. The PL peaks of Ni-CDs in different solvents were different.

For Ni-BCDs, the PL peaks shifted from 434 to 448 nm in the aprotonated solvent and from 449 to 461 nm in the protonated solvent. For Ni-GCDs, the PL peak was shifted from 512 to 532 nm in the aprotonated solvent while the peak shifted from 535 to 568 nm in the protonated solvent. Ni-CDs displayed solvent-dependent fluorescence characteristics.

The determination of water content in organic solvents has always been a hot topic in the chemical industry [45]. Karl Fischer titration can determine the water content of liquid and had been widely used in the past [46]. However, such method was much complicated. Because of the PL of Ni-CDs revealed dependence of polar solvent, Ni-CDs samples could be used for the determination of water content in organic solvents. Therefore, the PL evolution of Ni-CDs with H2O amount in organic solvents was tested. As well known, the polarity of water was the greatest compared with some organic solvents. The polarity of an organic solvent can be enhanced by increasing the water content. Introducing varying amounts of aqueous solutions to THF and ACN solutions enhanced the solvent polarity. Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples were dispersed in THF and ACN with different water volume ratios. The PL peaks of Ni-CDs were shifted. Fig. 7b and 7e show the PL evolution of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs with increasing the amount of H2O till to 100 %. The PL spectra of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were gradually red-shifted to with increasing the amount of H2O. In THF solution, the PL peak wavelengths of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples were redshifted from 448.6 to 512.6 to 460.6 and 565.6 nm, respectively. Samples Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs alsorevealed a red shift in ACN solutions. Compared with Ni-BCDs, Ni-GCDs samples exhibited high sensitivity in response to water in organic solvents. The insets in Fig. 7c and 7f demonstrated a significant alteration for the PL with the water volume ratio till to 10 % in THF. The blue-emitting of Ni-BCDs gradually lightened, and the green-emitting of Ni-GCDs changed to yellow-emitting. Then, the relationship of PL peak and water ratio was linearly as shown in Fig. 7c and 7f. In THF solution, the limitation of detection (LOD) of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were 0.015 and 0.0018 %, respectively. The slope difference is ascribed that Ni-CDs samples were preferentially solvated by the low polarity of a solvent mixture [47]. The result for Ni-CDs dispersed in ACN solution were similar with in THF solution. As shown in Fig. S4a and S4c, in ACN solution, the PL peak wavelengths of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples were red-shifted from 449.6 to 527.6 to 460.6 and 565.6 nm, respectively. Fig. S4b and S4d show PL peak positions revealed a well linear relationship with water ratios. The difference of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs dispersed in ACN solution with water ratios was very obviously. In ACN solution, the LOD of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were 0.029 and 0.009 %, respectively. These results may expend the application of emitting CDs.

To further demonstrate the effect of water on the PL of Ni-CDs, Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs were coated on filter paper to test the PL evolution before and after adding different amount of water in organic solvents as shown in Fig. S5 (see Supporting Information). Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, Ni-CDs on the filter paper revealed blue- and green-emitting after adding water. Obviously, the PL intensity of Ni-CDs was greatly increased. This confirms Ni-CDs revealed improved stability in solid powder situation.

Because Ni-GCDs exhibited excellent PL properties and high stability, a green-emitting conversion layer was prepared by embedding Ni-GCDs in PVA. Fig. 8 shows the synthesis procedure of Ni-GCDs films. Ni-CDs film was transparent under room light and exhibited bright green-emitting under UV light irradiation. Ni-GCDs film exhibited excellent flexibility nature as shown in the inset in Fig. 9 a. The PL peak of the Ni-GCDs film exhibited a blue shift compared with initial Ni-GCDs (Fig. 9a). The phenomenon is attributed to the impact of PVA on the PL properties of Ni-GCDs sample. Ni-GCDs film revealed high light stability.

The PL intensity of the film was maintained after irradiation using 365 nm UV light for 180 min (Fig. 9b). The Ni-GCDs film also exhibited long-term storage stability. After keeping in air condition 60 days, the PL intensity of the Ni-GCDs film remained at a high level (Fig. 9c). The Commission Internationale d'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates for Ni-GCDs film was (0.21, 0.41) (Fig. 9d). Ni-GCDs film with bright-emitting was highly suitable for incorporation into optoelectronic devices, particularly light-emitting diodes [48,49].

A one-step solvothermal synthesis was employed to create Ni-doped blue- and green-emitting CDs. Ni doping had been demonstrated as a highly effective method to enhance fluorescence properties of CDs. Ni-CDs samples exhibited high PLQY, narrower FWHM, stable and bright fluorescence characteristics and resistance to photobleaching. The PL peak wavelengths of Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples were 450 and 540 nm, respectively. The CDs and Ni-CDs samples exhibited behavior unrelated to excitation. Ni-BCDs and Ni-GCDs samples shown solvent dependence. With the increase of solvent polarity, the PL peak of Ni-CDs samples gradually redshifted. The solvent-dependent effect was attributed to the surface state of Ni-CDs samples, including the effects of solvent polarity and hydrogen bond formation. This work proved Ni-CDs samples had a promising application in determination of trace water in organic solvents. In addition, Ni-CDs embedded in PVA film revealed bright PL. The result indicates Ni-CDs with broad applications in light-emitting devices.

Xuanqi Hao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wenbin Shi: Methodology, Formal analysis. Ping Yang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the projects from the National Natural Science Function of China, PR China, (grant no. 51972145), the Independent Innovation Team from Ji Nan Science & Technology Bureau, Jinan, China (grant no, 2021GXRC109), and Science and Technology Program of University of Jinan, China (XKY2118).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.104592.
